metadata.title: Research the Negative Biopsy Problem with Claude: When Nothing Found Doesn't Mean Nothing There
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT(slate) · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Source: cancer-research/chapters/02-cancer-diagnosis-imaging-and-the-tissue-sample.md
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The biopsy was negative. The cancer was still there.

A pancreatic mass biopsy comes back benign. The oncologist considers the case closed. But imaging places pretest probability of malignancy at eighty percent. Should they be reassured? A negative biopsy does not mean no cancer — it means no cancer was found in this sample. How much that matters depends on the math that is rarely done at the bedside.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: 80% pretest probability; negative biopsy; the question about reassurance

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to compute the posterior probability using likelihood ratios, then name the three physical mechanisms for false negatives.

props.command:
claude "A pancreatic mass has pretest probability of
malignancy at 80%. EUS-FNA has sensitivity ~85%
for solid pancreatic masses. A negative biopsy
is returned. Use a likelihood-ratio approach to
compute the posterior probability of malignancy.
Then name the three physical mechanisms by which
a needle can return a false negative from a true
malignancy. State the clinical decision this
logic supports."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the math. Likelihood ratio negative equals one minus sensitivity over specificity — that is one minus point-eight-five over point-nine-nine, or zero-point-one-five. Pretest odds at eighty percent are four. Post-test odds are four times zero-point-one-five — zero-point-six. Convert back: probability equals zero-point-six over one-point-six — approximately forty-one percent.

props.code:
# bayesian_biopsy.py -- Claude Code output
SENS = 0.85   # EUS-FNA sensitivity, solid pancreatic mass
SPEC = 0.99   # specificity (few false positives)
PRE  = 0.80   # pretest probability from imaging

LR_neg = (1 - SENS) / SPEC         # = 0.152
pre_odds = PRE / (1 - PRE)          # = 4.0
post_odds = pre_odds * LR_neg       # = 0.606
post_prob = post_odds / (1 + post_odds)  # ≈ 0.377

print(f"LR-    = {LR_neg:.3f}")
print(f"Pre-test prob  = {PRE:.0%}")
print(f"Post-test prob = {post_prob:.1%}")
print()
# False-negative mechanisms:
# 1. Sampling miss: needle bypasses malignant tissue
# 2. Unrepresentative zone: necrotic/desmoplastic core sampled
# 3. Insufficient tissue: yield too low for pathologic diagnosis

beats.narration_text: The chart. Pretest probability: eighty percent — a tall crimson bar. After negative EUS-FNA: approximately forty-one percent. The bar falls but does not reach the ten-percent reference line. Ten percent is where the chapter's logic says you can consider stopping. Forty-one percent is still above it by a factor of four.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_BiopsyBars — two-bar before/after chart with reference line at 10%

beats.narration_text: Now re-run with a lower pretest probability — a breast lesion with thirty percent pretest — and ask at what posterior probability the logic supports accepting a negative biopsy.

props.command:
claude "Re-run with pretest probability of 30%
(BI-RADS 4A breast lesion). Compute the posterior
probability after a negative core biopsy (sensitivity
~97% for breast core). At what posterior probability
does the clinical logic support accepting a negative
biopsy result? What threshold does the chapter name?"

beats.narration_text: Breast core biopsy at ninety-seven percent sensitivity. Starting at thirty percent pretest, a negative result yields a posterior of approximately one percent. That is below ten percent — the logic supports accepting the result. The calculation, not the test name, determines whether you can close the case.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — breast core biopsy example: 30% pretest → 1% posterior; below 10% threshold; case can close

beats.narration_text: A negative biopsy does not mean no cancer. It means no cancer was found in this sample. How much reassurance that provides depends entirely on pretest probability and test sensitivity — arithmetic that is almost never done at the bedside. The three physical mechanisms for false negatives explain why sensitivity is never one hundred percent.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: negative ≠ absent; pretest × sensitivity determines reassurance value

beats.narration_text: Before accepting a negative biopsy result, compute the posterior. Find the test sensitivity for this specific modality and tumor type, set the pretest from imaging, run the likelihood-ratio calculation. If the posterior is above ten percent, the clinical logic supports repeat biopsy or multidisciplinary team review — not case closure.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: compute posterior; find sensitivity for modality; >10% means repeat or MDT

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
